section.title: He Who Dwells in the Shelter of the Most High
section.sectionRationale: A psalm of confident trust in God as refuge and fortress, this passage promises protection under the shadow of the Almighty and the guard of His angels, promises famously twisted by Satan when he quoted verses 11-12 to tempt Christ, whose refusal to test God shows how this psalm's true comfort is received through trust, not presumption.
section.thesis: The one who makes the Most High his dwelling place is promised protection from hidden traps, deadly plagues, and every terror, guarded by God's own angels, a promise Satan twisted at Christ's temptation but which Jesus embraced rightly, trusting the Father rather than testing Him, and modeling the true use of this psalm for every believer.
section.frameNote: Four movements: safe shelter under the Most High, verses 1-2; the LORD's delivering wings and shield, verses 3-8; the angelic escort promised to those who dwell in Him, verses 9-13; and God's own eightfold promise of deliverance, honor, and salvation, verses 14-16.

units.label: My Refuge and My Fortress
units.note: safe shelter under the Most High

verses.text: He who dwells in the {{machaseh|shelter}} of the {{shaddai|Most High}} will abide in the shadow of the Almighty.
I will say to the LORD, "You are my refuge and my fortress, my God, in whom I trust."

units.label: Delivering Wings and Shield
units.note: protection from hidden traps and terrors

verses.text: Surely He will deliver you from the {{pach|snare}} of the fowler, and from the deadly plague.
He will cover you with His feathers; under His wings you will find refuge; His faithfulness is a shield and rampart.
You will not fear the terror of the night, nor the arrow that flies by day,
nor the pestilence that stalks in the darkness, nor the calamity that destroys at noon.
Though a thousand may fall at your side, and ten thousand at your right hand, no harm will come near you.
You will only see it with your eyes and witness the punishment of the wicked.

units.label: He Will Command His Angels
units.note: the angelic escort of those who dwell in Him

verses.text: Because you have made the LORD your dwelling, my refuge, the Most High,
no evil will befall you, no plague will approach your tent.
For He will command His {{malak|angels}} concerning you to guard you in all your ways.
They will lift you up in their hands, so that you will not strike your foot against a stone.
You will tread on the lion and cobra; you will trample the young lion and serpent.
"Because he loves Me, I will deliver him; because he knows My name, I will protect him.
When he calls out to Me, I will answer him; I will be with him in trouble. I will deliver him and honor him.
With long life I will satisfy him and show him My salvation."

machaseh.gloss: Refuge, shelter, a place of safety. The psalm opens by describing the settled, abiding trust of one who makes this shelter, found in the Most High, his continual dwelling rather than a place he visits only in crisis.

shaddai.gloss: The Almighty, a name emphasizing God's all-sufficient power. Paired here with Most High, it grounds the psalm's promises of protection in the sheer, sufficient strength of God's character.

pach.gloss: A snare or trap, such as a bird-catcher used, made of reeds or cords. The image pictures hidden, deceptive dangers from which God promises to deliver the one who trusts Him.

malak.gloss: A messenger, an angel. God commands these heavenly messengers to guard His people, a promise Satan quoted out of context to tempt Christ to presumptuous testing rather than trusting obedience.

basic.context: This psalm pictures the safety of one who makes the Most High his continual dwelling place, promising deliverance from hidden traps, plagues, and every terror of night and day, guarded by God's own angels. Its most famous verses were quoted by Satan himself when he tempted Jesus in the wilderness.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm describes genuine, deep confidence available to those who trust God as their refuge; it does not prescribe presumptuous testing of God's promises, as Satan's misuse of verses 11-12 at Christ's temptation makes clear. True trust rests in God rather than manipulating His promises to avoid the normal path of obedience and suffering.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm opens with machaseh, shelter, and shaddai, the Almighty, verse 1, naming the settled place of trust available in God's all-sufficient power. It promises deliverance from the pach, snare, of the fowler, verse 3, and describes malak, angels, commanded to guard the one who dwells in God, verse 11.
basic.typology: Verse 13's promise, 'you will tread on the lion and cobra,' anticipates Christ's authority over Satan and every demonic power, and the ultimate crushing of the serpent under the feet of God's people, fulfilling the first promise of Genesis 3:15.
basic.christ: Satan quoted this psalm's promise of angelic protection, verses 11-12, to tempt Jesus to throw Himself from the temple and force God's hand, Matthew 4:6. Jesus refused, showing that this psalm's comfort is received through trust and obedience, not through testing God, and that true security is found in walking the Father's appointed path, even one that led to the cross.
basic.god: God is shown as refuge, fortress, and shield, one who genuinely delivers His people from real dangers, yet who is never to be tested or manipulated, even by quoting His own promises out of context. He answers when His people call, is present with them in trouble, and promises ultimate salvation.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 4:5-7 and Luke 4:9-12 record Satan quoting this psalm to tempt Christ, and Jesus' refusal to test God. Luke 10:19 shows Christ giving His disciples authority to tread on serpents and scorpions. Romans 16:20 promises that God will soon crush Satan underfoot.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's promise of refuge and angelic protection, twisted once by Satan to tempt Christ, is rightly received through trust rather than presumption, and finds its fullest meaning in Jesus, who walked the path of obedient suffering and now shares His authority over every serpent and lion with those who dwell in Him.

context.literary: A wisdom psalm of confident trust, moving from a general statement of the blessing of dwelling in God, verses 1-2, through vivid promises of protection, verses 3-13, to God's own direct speech affirming His covenant promises, verses 14-16.
context.historical: Likely composed for use in worship or for pilgrims and soldiers facing real dangers, its imagery of plague, ambush, and battle reflects the genuine hazards of ancient Near Eastern life, from which the psalm promises God's covenant protection.
context.canonical: Its most famous verses became a flashpoint in redemptive history when Satan quoted them to tempt Christ, making this psalm a key text for understanding the difference between genuine trust in God's promises and presumptuous manipulation of them.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure God's people of genuine protection and care for those who make Him their dwelling place, while implicitly requiring that this trust be lived out through faithful obedience rather than through testing or manipulating God's promises.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes the real, deep security available to those who trust God; it does not prescribe using its promises as a guarantee against all suffering or as license to act recklessly, since Christ Himself refused to use it this way when tempted.

debates.question: Does this psalm promise believers will never suffer harm, or does it promise something deeper than the absence of suffering?

views.label: It promises ultimate, not necessarily immediate, deliverance and vindication
views.case: Many faithful believers throughout history have suffered greatly, even to martyrdom, showing that the psalm's promises must refer to God's ultimate care and final salvation rather than a guarantee against all present harm.

views.label: It promises real, often immediate protection for those who trust God, understood within the normal boundaries of wise and obedient living
views.case: The psalm's vivid, specific language about plagues, arrows, and ambush suggests genuine expectation of God's protective care in ordinary dangers, without implying a magical exemption from the normal call to suffer for righteousness.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that this psalm's comfort is for those who trust and obey God, not for those who test Him presumptuously, as Christ's own example in refusing Satan's misuse of this text makes definitively clear.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm's opening promise rests on machaseh, shelter, and shaddai, the Almighty, verse 1, the all-sufficient power of God as the ground of confident trust. It pictures the pach, snare, of the fowler, verse 3, from which God delivers, and malak, angels, commanded to guard His own, verse 11, the very promise Satan later misapplied at Christ's temptation.

typology.type: Treading on the lion and cobra, trampling the young lion and serpent, verse 13
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ's authority over Satan and every demonic power, Luke 10:19, and the final crushing of the serpent under God's people, Romans 16:20, echoing Genesis 3:15.

typology.type: The angelic guard commanded over God's own, verses 11-12
typology.fulfillment: Cited by Satan to tempt Christ, Matthew 4:6, yet rightly fulfilled as angels ministered to Jesus after His temptation, Matthew 4:11, and continue to serve those who will inherit salvation, Hebrews 1:14.

study.christ: Satan quoted this psalm's promise of angelic protection to tempt Jesus to leap from the temple and force God's hand, Matthew 4:5-6. Jesus answered, 'You shall not put the Lord your God to the test,' refusing to twist this psalm's comfort into presumption. His obedience, walking the path of genuine suffering rather than demanding rescue, shows that this psalm's true security is found not in escaping hardship but in trusting the Father through it, exactly as Christ did on the way to the cross before His resurrection vindication.
study.god: God is refuge, fortress, and shield, genuinely present with His people in trouble and able to deliver them, yet He is never to be tested or manipulated. He answers those who call on Him, honors those who love Him, and ultimately shows His salvation to those who trust Him rather than presume upon Him.

crossReferences.note: Satan quotes this psalm's promise of angelic protection to tempt Christ, who refuses to test God, showing the difference between trust and presumption.
Jesus gives His disciples authority to tread on serpents and scorpions, echoing verse 13's promise.
God will soon crush Satan under the feet of His people, the ultimate fulfillment of treading on the serpent.

study.oneStory: This psalm's promise of refuge under the Most High, twisted once by Satan to tempt Christ toward presumption, is answered rightly by Jesus' obedient trust rather than by testing God, and it points forward to the full authority over every serpent and lion that Christ now shares with those who make Him their dwelling place.

sermon.title: Trusting God in Difficult Times, Psalm 91
sermon.why: In this message from his series for difficult times, Keller shows that Psalm 91 does not promise Christians will avoid all hardship, but rather that God's presence and ultimate deliverance are certain, correcting the very misuse of this psalm that Satan attempted against Christ.

quiz.q: What does it mean to 'dwell in the shelter of the Most High,' according to verse 1?

options.text: To make God one's continual, settled place of refuge and trust, not merely a place visited in crisis.
options.note: The verb 'dwells' pictures ongoing residence, a settled trust rather than occasional reliance.

options.text: To live inside the physical temple building at all times.
options.note: The shelter described is spiritual and relational, trust in God Himself, not a literal building requirement.

options.text: To avoid all contact with other people.
options.note: The psalm describes trust in God, not isolation from others.

options.text: To seek God's protection primarily during times of crisis.
options.note: This captures part of the psalm's promise, but verse 1 pictures continual, settled dwelling in God, not protection sought only in emergencies.

quiz.q: What promise from this psalm did Satan quote to tempt Jesus?

options.text: That God would command His angels to guard Him, so that His foot would not strike a stone.
options.note: Satan quoted verses 11-12 to tempt Jesus to leap from the temple and test God's protection.

options.text: That God would give Him all the kingdoms of the world.
options.note: That temptation is recorded separately in the Gospels and is not drawn from this psalm.

options.text: That God would turn stones into bread.
options.note: That temptation does not draw on this psalm, but on Jesus' hunger after fasting.

options.text: That God's protection was unconditional and could be tested at will.
options.note: This captures the spirit of Satan's twisted use of the psalm, though the specific verses quoted were 11-12, about angels guarding against striking one's foot on a stone.

quiz.q: How did Jesus respond to Satan's misuse of this psalm?

options.text: He refused to test God, showing that trust, not presumption, is the psalm's true use.
options.note: Jesus answered that one must not put the Lord God to the test, correcting Satan's twisted application.

options.text: He agreed and threw Himself from the temple.
options.note: Jesus refused this temptation rather than acting on Satan's misapplication.

options.text: He denied that the psalm was Scripture.
options.note: Jesus never denied the psalm's authority; He corrected its misuse, not its validity.

options.text: He quoted a different Scripture emphasizing trust rather than testing.
options.note: This is essentially correct in substance, Deuteronomy 6:16, though His precise words were that one must not put the Lord your God to the test.

quiz.q: What does verse 13 promise about the lion, cobra, and serpent?

options.text: That the one who trusts God will tread on and trample them.
options.note: This verse pictures triumph over dangerous and deceptive powers, fulfilled ultimately in Christ's authority over evil.

options.text: That these creatures will multiply and overwhelm God's people.
options.note: The verse promises victory over these dangers, not their multiplication or triumph.

options.text: That God's people should avoid wilderness areas entirely.
options.note: The verse is about authority and victory over danger, not geographic avoidance.

options.text: That believers will have supernatural courage in dangerous places.
options.note: This is a fair general sense of the verse's promise, though the specific picture given is treading on and trampling the lion, cobra, and serpent.

quiz.q: What eightfold promise does God make in verses 14-16?

options.text: Deliverance, protection, answered prayer, presence in trouble, rescue, honor, long life, and salvation.
options.note: These closing verses list God's own direct promises to the one who loves Him and knows His name.

options.text: Wealth, fame, political power, and military conquest.
options.note: The promises named are spiritual and relational, not worldly wealth or conquest.

options.text: A guarantee of never experiencing any trouble.
options.note: Verse 15 explicitly says 'I will be with him in trouble,' assuming trouble may come, not that it will be entirely absent.

options.text: Assurance that God will always answer prayer immediately.
options.note: This is close to true, verse 15, though the fuller promise also includes deliverance, honor, long life, and salvation, not answered prayer alone.

questions: The psalm describes one who 'dwells' in God's shelter, not merely one who visits it occasionally. What would it look like for you to make God your continual, settled refuge rather than an emergency resource?
Satan twisted this psalm's promise to tempt Jesus toward presumption. Have you ever been tempted to demand that God prove Himself rather than simply trusting Him through a hard season?
Jesus refused to test God even though He could have quoted this very psalm to justify it. What does His example teach you about the difference between genuine faith and presumption?
Verse 15 promises, 'I will be with him in trouble,' not the absence of trouble. How does knowing God's promise is presence in trouble, not exemption from it, change how you pray during hardship?

This psalm closes with God's own eightfold promise of care. Which of these eight promises, deliverance, protection, answered prayer, presence, rescue, honor, long life, or salvation, do you most need to remember today?

translationNotes.note: Most High and Almighty translate two distinct Hebrew divine titles, Elyon and Shaddai, both emphasizing God's supreme power and sufficiency.
The snare of the fowler pictures the baited traps ancient bird-catchers used, a vivid image for hidden schemes and unseen dangers.
These verses are directly quoted by Satan in Matthew 4:6 and Luke 4:10-11 during Christ's temptation, making this psalm a key text for distinguishing biblical trust from presumptuous testing of God.

meta.notes: BSB text obtained via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com's BSB (Berean Standard Bible), cross-checked against the section map bounds (Psalm 91:1-16, 16 verses). Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Tim Keller's message 'Trusting God in Difficult Times, Psalm 91' at podcast.gospelinlife.com.
